result:
I often wonder what it would feel like to inhabit silence, the kind that exists long before a ringing bell has been struck and long after its last vibration drowns in air. Not the heroic silence of desert hermits or glacier explorers, but the mid-afternoon, commonplace hush that sneaks into the corner of an overheated library the very moment the fluorescents forget their hum. I have chased it the way one chases the memory of childhood perfume, rummaging through hourly routine, tugging at the loose threads of obligations—meetings, bills, planetary terrors—until my pockets are full of meaningless lint and the bell has never been struck at all.

When I was small, silence happened by accident. Half a minute before my mother’s key clicked in the door, the air thickened as if an invisible bell jar sealed the parquet floor. I was meant to be practicing piano scales; instead I’d been designing sprawling frontier towns with dominoes. Instantly, the colorful cardboard boxes of sound snapped shut. My breath was try-hard and guilty, my ears raw antennae searching for the approaching footsteps. That mini-apocalypse of waiting—the sudden awareness of heart, pulse, and clock—was my first taste of intentional silence. It felt tingly, slightly criminal, like lukewarm water in July when everyone says don’t swallow.  

Years later, in a mid-rise apartment with radiator valves that hiccuped all winter, I learned how silence could be manufactured through subtraction. I came home from work and toured the rooms methodically, turning every knob counter-clock: refrigerator compressor, desktop fan, buzzing bulb in the hallway. I even unplugged the microwave so its digital zero wouldn’t glow like an accusation. Once the devices shut their little mouths, I sat on the crimson rug my grandmother wove before her cataracts blurred the colors, and listened in quadraphonic darkness.

The silence that arrived was not emptiness, not absence—I’d expected that from other people’s poems—but a crowded square. In it trudged grandfather clocks from houses I’d never entered; somewhere a wooden wheel rasped haltingly. Train whistles older than railroads migrated upriver; a page from an accounting ledger muttered its own self-immolation. The deal, I soon realized, is that when you lower the volume of life today, the ghosts of all yesterdays burn brighter, the way quarks catch sunlight at the edge of midnight accelerators. If you sit long enough in radiant hush, the ancestors line up like lonely icicles tapping on windows.

Most days now I carry a coil of stillness somewhere between the bones of inner ear and the back of my skull, the way a pink petal adheres to a streetem> grocery list. I unroll it at unpredictable moments: waiting at the long stoplight where commuters tap steering wheels as if keeping time for dancing skeletons; or during customer-on-hold music that pretends to be bossa nova. When the rain drills the awning of the bakery, I hear underneath the cloudburst a minor third the piano teacher once called “the sadness of a half-finished biscuit.”  

It was only recently I noticed I’d begun training other people to sense it, too. My niece, age seven and disbelieving, reported that Uncle’s house contains a pocket where Wi-Fi can’t fit. She whisper-screamed that my kitchen clock does not tick, except when we blink. I want to tell her observation lightly, but it’s unsettling how things bark when they finally feel witnessed.  

Some evenings I revisit that same apartment, though it no longer belongs to me. The building was sold and painted a muted gray that looks oddly porous, as if it plans to absorb the city one dusk at a time. On lucky nights, the new tenants leave the lobby window cracked, and the radiator chorus begins mid-aria. I linger on the stoop under the pretext of lacing a shoe and let the draft announce: You cannot step twice into the same hush. The syllables are altered by wind, by the intern who sings leakily as she locks up a cellphone kiosk, by basketballs that strike asphalt like muted metronomes. Yet there remains a residue, microscopic, capsaicin-level atomic—memories don’t abandon architecture as cleanly as landlords hope.

Yesterday my phone rang during one of these stoop sojourns, and I answered with reflexive guilt, the way one used to answer rotary phones tethered to a kitchen wall covered in soft oatmeal paint. The caller was an algorithm reminding me to renew my audiobook subscription. On the other end the synthesized voice was smooth, almost contrite, offering me a guided tour through “the authoritative library of human hush”—recordings of lava caves, Tibetan monasteries, pre-dawn snowfields in Hokkaido. I declined, which felt absurd, like refusing oxygen, but what I crave isn’t archive-ready silence; it’s the kind that still has a mouth full of bindweed and alley cats, a silence that interrupts itself because the universe hasn’t yet agreed to be curated.

At night, dreams serve contraband evidence of this craving. I find myself standing in an abandoned amusement park where all the rides rust clockwise in silence so obliterive I lose the memory of music. Coconut oil from funnel cake joints has congealed around the ticket stalls like bright grammar tears. A single streetlamp buzzes, but the bulb itself is screamingly dark; its filament remembers how to vibrate but has lost the alphabet of light. I walk toward the Ferris wheel and discover every car holds a sleeping child encased in gently in glass—glass made of every unanswered message, every shook-then-canceled plan. There is no operator. Instead, a pair of pigeons cockpit the apex, taking turns pecking invisible controls. Their wing-beats are deaf yet rattling: a pulse that turns the wheel precisely one notch per dream cycle, slow as the moon measuring grief.

I wake each time the wheel aligns the ground cage with the gate where daylight tries to reenter. It’s easy afterward to yawn and blame late-night tacos, but part of me believes the park is factual, or at least more factual than the stock price scrolling across my phone screen.<|reserved_token_163802|>

In such moments I return to the rug—same red, now sun-bleached coral, positioned by habit at the foot of another rented bed. I sit, cross-legged, not intending to meditate, only to balance on the threadbare edge of the seen and the heard. The radiator here behaves, the neighbors are merciful strangers. A lone cricket is pressing violin hips into late summer. Then, between its chirrs, I hear the library again—that mid-afternoon ghost—only this time fluorescence is replaced by a soft ticking that grows quieter the closer I lean. I can almost name it, almost place it, when I realize the sound is coming from the center of me: the metronome of thinking has not stopped, but has grown obedient, filing its notices in order.

What astonishes me next is how forgiving it feels. The thought-ticks are small, four-note sentences seeking harmony with the cricket legato outside the screen. Together they occupy the air like two halves of a wishbone, neither claiming victory. There is no crescendo, no revelation gong. The rooftop merely holds the night, the cricket arcs an eyebrow of music, and the hush folds and unfolds itself like fabric I forgot I owned. This is where the essay usually ends: a cinematic fade to black. But the fascinating thing is that nothing fades. Something in the dark negotiates another breath, and another, knitting the visible and audible into a single fabric too humble for cathedral brass—more like a dishtowel drying, ready tomorrow to be ruffled by mere fingers doing ordinary work.

And in that unheroic continuation I finally sense how quiet becomes ordinary, as invisible and nourishing as salt dissolved in soup. One could live an entire life chasing the bell before it rings, missing the fact that silence is as close as the next inhale, wrapped around the cartilage of daily breathing like the silk paper my grandmother used to fold around wedding almonds—protective, fragrant of burnt sugar, infinitely recyclable.

So tonight I plug the microwave back in. I let its tiny display count midnight in luminous quadrants. I sit and listen until the zeroes, the cricket, the unnamed radiator, and my hesitant blood arrange themselves into one resonance only I can mispronounce: silence, pronounced like “home.”

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
